1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.

5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	\chapter{The Quot scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the Quot-foundations layer of the
6	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
7	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\): the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial, the
8	% Quot functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on
9	% \(X_T = X \times_S T\), and the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} together with
10	% its representability. The Quot functor underlies the relative Picard
11	% functor via the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.
13	%
14	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The Quot construction embeds Quot as a
15	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
16	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
17	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
19	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
20	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
21	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability
22	% (\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme

55	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}

57	\begin{definition}\leanok
58	[Hilbert polynomial]
59	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
61	  \uses{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}

85	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special

87	  %
88	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
89	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
90	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
91	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
92	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
93	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
94	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
95	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
96	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)

101	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
102	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
103	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), write \(X_s\)
104	  for the fibre over \(s\), \(\mathcal{F}_s = \mathcal{F}|_{X_s}\) and
105	  \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\). The \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\)
106	  relative to \(L\)} is the unique polynomial
107	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) that agrees, for all
108	  \(m \gg 0\), with the graded Hilbert function
109	  \[
110	    m \;\longmapsto\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr)
111	  \]
112	  of the section module
113	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
114	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), viewed as a finitely generated graded module
115	  over the homogeneous section ring
116	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
117	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}). Existence and uniqueness of
118	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) are the content of
119	  \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}.
120	
121	  This is the classical Hilbert polynomial. When \(\mathcal{F}_s\) is coherent
122	  and \(L_s\) is ample on the proper \(\kappa(s)\)-scheme \(X_s\), Serre vanishing
123	  gives \(H^i(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\)
124	  and \(m \gg 0\), so for \(m \gg 0\)
125	  \[
126	    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
127	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
128	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}),
129	  \]
130	  and hence \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) coincides with the Euler-characteristic
131	  Hilbert polynomial of Snapper's Lemma -- no numerical invariant is lost. The
132	  construction above, however, uses only the global sections \(H^0\): the higher
133	  cohomology enters solely through the classical statement that the two functions
134	  agree for \(m \gg 0\), never through the definition of \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\)
135	  itself. When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
136	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
137	  well-defined on connected components.
138	\end{definition}

140	\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
141	\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}
142	
143	This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
144	\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
145	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
146	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
147	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
148	(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
149	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
150	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
151	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
152	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
153	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.
154	
155	\begin{definition}
156	[Section graded ring]
157	  \label{def:sectionGradedRing}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing}
159	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
160	  % sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials" (read from
161	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
162	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
163	  % "Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
164	  %  a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
165	  %  Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
166	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
167	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
168	  %  the function
169	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
170	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
171	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
172	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
173	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the section graded
174	  ring is the homogeneous coordinate ring of [Hartshorne], I.7.}
175	  Let \(s \in S\) and let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on the fibre \(X_s\), a scheme
176	  of finite type over the residue field \(\kappa(s)\). The \emph{section graded
177	  ring} of \(L_s\) is the graded commutative ring
178	  \[
179	    R(X_s, L_s) \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
180	  \]
181	  with degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and multiplication
182	  the tensor product of sections
183	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
184	  \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'}) \to \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\),
185	  \(\sigma \otimes \tau \mapsto \sigma \cdot \tau\). Its degree-zero component is
186	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s})\), which contains \(\kappa(s)\). When \(X_s\) is
187	  proper over \(\kappa(s)\) and \(L_s\) is ample, \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely
188	  generated -- hence Noetherian -- graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra (a suitable
189	  Veronese power of \(L_s\) is very ample and embeds \(X_s\) into a projective
190	  space, exhibiting \(R(X_s, L_s)\) up to a finite extension as a quotient of a
191	  polynomial ring).
192	\end{definition}
193	
194	\begin{definition}
195	[Section graded module]
196	  \label{def:sectionGradedModule}
197	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule}
198	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing}
199	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
200	  % Polynomials" (read from
201	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
202	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
203	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
204	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
205	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
206	  %  the function
207	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
208	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
209	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
210	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
211	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
212	  I.7.}
213	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on the fibre \(X_s\) and \(L_s\) a
214	  line bundle on \(X_s\). The \emph{section graded module} of \(\mathcal{F}_s\)
215	  twisted by \(L_s\) is the graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module
216	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing})
217	  \[
218	    M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)
219	    \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
220	  \]
221	  with degree-\(m\) component the \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space
222	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) and scalar action given
223	  by the section tensor product
224	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
225	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
226	  \to \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\). Its graded Hilbert
227	  function is \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)}
228	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
229	\end{definition}

231	\begin{lemma}
232	[Finite generation of the section graded module]
233	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
234	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_fg}
235	  \uses{def:sectionGradedModule, def:sectionGradedRing}
236	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
237	  % Polynomials" (read from
238	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
239	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
240	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
241	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
242	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
243	  %  the function
244	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
245	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
246	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
247	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
248	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Serre's theorem on
249	  ample line bundles, [Hartshorne], I.7 / II.5.}
250	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
251	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
252	  Then the section graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) is a
253	  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
254	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
255	  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
256	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
257	  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.
258	\end{lemma}
259	
260	\begin{proof}
261	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule}
262	  Since \(L_s\) is ample and \(X_s\) is proper over \(\kappa(s)\), a Veronese
263	  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
264	  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
265	  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
266	  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
267	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
268	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
269	  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
270	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
271	  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
272	  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
273	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
274	  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
275	  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).
276	\end{proof}

278	\begin{lemma}[Existence and uniqueness of the Hilbert polynomial of a rational
279	  graded series]
280	  \label{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
281	  \lean{Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly}
282	  \mathlibok
283	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
284	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Polynomial.HilbertPoly}).}
285	  Let \(F\) be a field of characteristic zero, let \(p \in F[X]\), and let
286	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then there is a unique polynomial \(h \in F[X]\) such that
287	  for some \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) and all \(n > N\) the coefficient of \(X^n\) in the
288	  power-series expansion of \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \in F\llbracket X\rrbracket\)
289	  equals \(h(n)\):
290	  \[
291	    \exists!\, h \in F[X],\ \exists N,\ \forall n > N : \;
292	    \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1-X)^{-d} \bigr) \;=\; h(n).
293	  \]
294	  Equivalently, the eventual coefficient function of any rational power series
295	  with denominator a power of \(1 - X\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), given by a unique
296	  polynomial \(h\) (Mathlib's \(\mathrm{hilbertPoly}\,p\,d\)). The hypothesis
297	  \([\mathrm{CharZero}\,F]\) is satisfied by \(F = \mathbb{Q}\), which is the
298	  instance used to obtain \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\).
299	\end{lemma}
300	
301	\begin{lemma}[Graded Hilbert--Serre: rationality of the Hilbert series]
302	  \label{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}
303	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational}
304	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule, lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
305	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
306	  % Polynomials" (read from
307	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L459-L464).
308	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
309	  % "$${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
310	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
311	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
312	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that $\chi(F(m))$
313	  %  is indeed a polynomial in $m$ under the above assumption is a special
314	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof)."
315	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Hilbert--Serre,
316	  [Hartshorne], I.7.}
317	  Let \(R\) be a Noetherian graded ring whose degree-zero part is a field
318	  \(\kappa\) and which is generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra by finitely many
319	  elements, and let \(M\) be a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module with
320	  finite-dimensional graded components \(M_n\). Then there exist
321	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
322	  such that for all \(n > N\)
323	  \[
324	    \dim_{\kappa} M_n \;=\; \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr);
325	  \]
326	  that is, the Hilbert series \(\sum_{n \ge 0} (\dim_\kappa M_n)\, X^n\) is, up to
327	  a polynomial correction in low degrees, the rational function
328	  \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\). Applied to \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and
329	  \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (finitely generated by
330	  \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), this exhibits the graded Hilbert function
331	  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
332	  as the eventual coefficient function of such a rational series.
333	
334	  \emph{Status.} This is the substantive half of the graded Hilbert--Serre
335	  theorem (rationality of the Hilbert series of a finitely generated graded
336	  module). It is a project-side obligation: Mathlib's
337	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} supplies only the converse
338	  polynomial-extraction step from a rational series, not the production of the
339	  rational series from the module.
340	\end{lemma}
341	
342	\begin{proof}
343	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
344	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the ring \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) is Noetherian
345	  and finitely generated over \(\kappa = \kappa(s)\), say by homogeneous
346	  generators \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) of positive degrees, and
347	  \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
348	  with finite-dimensional components. Passing to a Veronese subalgebra and
349	  re-grading reduces to the case where \(R\) is generated in degree \(1\), so
350	  \(R\) is a graded quotient of a polynomial ring
351	  \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\). One proves rationality of the Hilbert series by
352	  induction on \(r\): multiplication by \(x_r\) gives a short exact sequence of
353	  graded modules
354	  \(0 \to K \to M(-1) \xrightarrow{x_r} M \to C \to 0\)
355	  with \(K, C\) finitely generated graded modules annihilated by \(x_r\), hence
356	  modules over a polynomial ring in \(r - 1\) variables; additivity of
357	  \(\dim_\kappa\) on the graded components turns the inductive hypothesis for
358	  \(K, C\) into the statement that
359	  \((1 - X) \cdot \sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n\) is, up to a polynomial, of the
360	  form \(q \cdot (1 - X)^{-(d-1)}\), whence
361	  \(\sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n = p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\) up to a polynomial. The
362	  base case \(r = 0\) has \(M\) finite-dimensional, so \(\dim_\kappa M_n = 0\) for
363	  \(n \gg 0\) and the series is a polynomial. Choosing \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\)
364	  (the integer coefficients viewed in \(\mathbb{Q}\)) gives the claim.
365	\end{proof}

367	\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
368	  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
369	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
370	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
371	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
372	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
373	  % Polynomials" (read from
374	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
375	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
376	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
377	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
378	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
379	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
380	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
381	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
382	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
383	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
384	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
385	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
386	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
387	  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
388	  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
389	  \[
390	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
391	    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
392	  \]
393	  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
394	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
395	\end{theorem}
396	
397	\begin{proof}
398	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
399	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
400	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
401	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
402	  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components. By
403	  \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} there are \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
404	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
405	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
406	  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
407	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
408	  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
409	  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
410	  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
411	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
412	  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
413	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
414	  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
415	  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
416	  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
417	\end{proof}

469	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
470	  \(L\) a line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\), and
471	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). The \emph{Quot functor with Hilbert
472	  polynomial \(\Phi\)}
473	  \[
474	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set}
475	  \]
476	  sends an \(S\)-scheme \(T \to S\) to the set of equivalence classes
477	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) where
478	  \begin{enumerate}
479	    \item \(\mathcal{F}\) is a coherent sheaf on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) whose
480	      schematic support is proper over \(T\) and which is flat over \(T\);
481	    \item \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) is a surjective
482	      \(\mathcal{O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism, where \(E_T\) is the pull-back
483	      of \(E\) along \(X_T \to X\);
484	    \item for every \(t \in T\) the Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}\)
485	      with respect to \(L|_{X_t}\) (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}) equals
486	      \(\Phi\).
487	  \end{enumerate}
488	  Two pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) and \((\mathcal{F}', q')\) are equivalent iff
489	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). On morphisms \(f : T' \to T\) over \(S\), the functor
490	  acts by pulling back the quotient along \(X_{T'} \to X_T\) (well-defined
491	  since tensor product is right-exact and preserves both flatness and
492	  properness). The Hilbert scheme is the special case
493	  \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} := \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\).
494	\end{definition}

496	\section{The Grassmannian scheme}
497	\label{sec:grassmannian_scheme}
498	
499	The Quot construction proceeds by embedding \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) as a
500	locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian over \(S\). Since Mathlib carries
501	no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} (only a finite-rank-quotient variety in
502	linear algebra), we package the construction here.
503	
504	\begin{definition}\leanok
505	[Grassmannian scheme]
506	  \label{def:grassmannian_scheme}
507	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian}
508	  \uses{def:quot_functor}

515	  %  the Grassmannian scheme
516	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\), together with the tautological
517	  %  quotient \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) where \
518	  %  \(\U\) is a rank \(d\) locally free sheaf on \(\grass(r,d)\),
519	  %  has been given at the end of this section. [...]
520	  %  Show that \(\grass(r,d)\) together with the quotient
521	  %  \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) represents the
522	  %  contravariant functor
523	  %  \(\)\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}\(\)
524	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any \(T\) the
525	  %  set of all equivalence classes

527	  %  where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\).
528	  %  [...]
529	  %  Using the above show that, more generally, if \(S\) is a scheme and
530	  %  \(E\) is a locally free \({\mathcal O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\),
531	  %  the functor \(\Grass(E,d)=\Quot_{E/S/S}^{d,{\mathcal O}_S}\)
532	  %  on all \(S\)-schemes which
533	  %  by definition associates to any \(T\)
534	  %  the set of all equivalence classes \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients
535	  %  \(q: E_T \to \F\) where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\),
536	  %  is representable. The representing scheme
537	  %  is denoted by \(\grass(E,d)\) and is called the rank \(d\)
538	  %  relative Grassmannian of \(E\) over \(S\)."

540	  Grassmannian" (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
541	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(V\) be a locally free
542	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\). For an integer \(1 \le d \le r\), the
543	  \emph{Grassmannian of rank-\(d\) quotients of \(V\)} is the functor
544	  \[
545	    \mathrm{Grass}(V, d) : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set},
546	    \qquad
547	    T \longmapsto \{ \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle :
548	    q : V_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F},\; \mathcal{F}
549	    \text{ locally free of rank } d \}
550	  \]
551	  with equivalence \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Concretely \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d) =
552	  \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}\) (the Quot functor for \(X = S\), \(E = V\),
553	  constant Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi = d\)).
554	
555	  Structurally \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d)\) is \emph{defined} as this Quot functor
556	  (\cref{def:quot_functor}) of the special case \(X = S\), \(E = V\),
557	  \(\Phi = d\) -- not as a second, independently-built functor. Fixing the
558	  definition this way makes the identity
559	  \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d) = \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}\) hold by
560	  construction, so the structural correspondence is preserved automatically
561	  when the underlying \(\Quot\) construction is later filled in, and the
562	  Grassmannian inherits its functoriality and equivalence relation from the
563	  Quot functor rather than restating them.
564	\end{definition}

677	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the "Construction of Grassmannian" sub-section of
678	% [Nitsure], \S 1 (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
679	% L773-L930), which gives the construction in five named steps
680	% ("Construction by gluing together affine patches", "Separatedness",
681	% "Properness", "Universal quotient", "Projective embedding"). The total
682	% verbatim text is ~150 lines; it is reproduced piecewise in the
683	% SOURCE QUOTE comment of the theorem statement above.
684	\begin{proof}
685	  
686	  Over \(S = \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) and \(V = \mathcal{O}_S^{\oplus r}\) the
687	  Grassmannian \(\mathrm{Gr}(r, d)\) is constructed by gluing
688	  \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) indexed by
689	  size-\(d\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\). The chart \(U^I\)
690	  carries the matrix \(X^I\) of size \(d \times r\) whose \(I\)-th \(d \times d\)
691	  minor is the identity, with the remaining \(d(r-d)\) entries free
692	  variables (so \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)). The
693	  transition maps \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I\) defined
694	  by \(X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) on the locus where \(\det X^I_J\) is
695	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
696	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\). The glued
697	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec\mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
698	  \(d(r-d)\). Separatedness is checked from the diagonal
699	  \(\Delta_{I,J} \subset U^I \times U^J\) cut out by \(X^J_I X^I - X^J = 0\).
700	  Properness follows from the valuative criterion for DVRs: given
701	  \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over a DVR \(R\) with fraction
702	  field \(K\), factoring through some chart \(U^I\) via a ring map
703	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\), choose \(J\) minimising the valuation
704	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) of the minor; on the chart \(U^J\) all entries have
705	  non-negative valuation, so \(f\) extends uniquely to \(\Spec R\).
706	
707	  The universal quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
708	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) is given on \(U^I\) by the matrix \(X^I\)
709	  and glued by \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)\). The
710	  determinant line bundle \(\det\mathcal{U}\) has transition functions
711	  \(\det g_{I,J} = 1/P^I_J\); the global sections
712	  \(\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), \det\mathcal{U})\) defined by
713	  \(\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I\) form a base-point-free linear system separating
714	  points over \(\Spec\mathbb{Z}\), giving an embedding into
715	  \(\mathbb{P}^{\binom{r}{d}-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\), which is closed by
716	  properness. Representability of \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d)\) is then verified
717	  on the universal pair \((\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), u)\): a quotient
718	  \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\oplus r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of
719	  rank \(d\) on \(T\) is everywhere locally trivialised; on the locus where
720	  the columns indexed by \(I\) are a basis the matrix of \(q\) has the form
721	  \(X^I\), defining the classifying morphism \(T \to U^I \subset
722	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Base change from \(\mathbb{Z}\) to a general noetherian
723	  base \(S\) with a vector bundle \(V\) is direct: trivialise \(V\) on an open
724	  cover and glue. For non-trivial \(V\) the same construction produces
725	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) with Pl\"ucker embedding into
726	  \(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\).
727	\end{proof}

729	\section{Lean encoding}
730	\label{sec:quot_lean_encoding}
731	
732	The Lean target file is
733	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean}. The Quot-foundations
734	layer proceeds in two phases.
735	
736	\paragraph{Phase 1: Hilbert polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}).}
737	On a finite-type morphism \(\pi : X \to S\) with \(S\) noetherian and a
738	coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) with proper support over \(S\), the per-fiber
739	Hilbert polynomial is encoded as a function \(s \mapsto
740	\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). Mathlib provides
741	Euler-characteristic infrastructure on coherent sheaves on noetherian
742	schemes; the polynomial property of
743	\(m \mapsto \chi(\mathcal{F} \otimes L^{\otimes m})\) requires
744	Snapper's Lemma, which is a project-side sub-build on top of the
745	graded-Euler-characteristic infrastructure.
746	
747	\emph{Intended signature of \(\mathrm{hilbertPolynomial}\).} Mathlib at the pin
748	carries no single \texttt{IsCoherent} predicate; coherence of \(\mathcal{F}\)
749	over the locally noetherian base is the conjunction of the two predicates that
750	do exist, \texttt{SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent} and
751	\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType} -- i.e.\ the hypotheses
752	\([\mathcal{F}.\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}]\) and
753	\([\mathcal{F}.\mathrm{IsFiniteType}]\) (and likewise for the line bundle
754	\(L\)). The properness-of-support hypothesis
755	``\(\operatorname{supp}\mathcal{F}\) proper over \(S\)'' has \emph{no} clean
756	Mathlib predicate. The minimal honest encoding forms the schematic support of
757	\(\mathcal{F}\) as a closed subscheme \(Z \hookrightarrow X\) and requires the
758	composite \(Z \hookrightarrow X \xrightarrow{\pi} S\) to satisfy
759	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper}; since the schematic-support closed
760	subscheme of a coherent sheaf is not itself available at the pin, this support
761	datum is recorded as a project-side gap to be supplied alongside the
762	definition. (The existing Lean pin
763	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial} is retained; adding these
764	hypotheses to its signature is a later refactor of the \texttt{.lean} stub, not
765	done in this pass.)
766	
767	\paragraph{Phase 2: Quot and Grassmannian functors
768	(\cref{def:quot_functor}, \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme},
769	\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).} The Quot functor
770	\(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) is encoded as a contravariant functor on
771	\((\mathrm{Sch}/S)\) of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients; the Grassmannian
772	\(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d)\) is the special case \(X = S\), \(E = V\), constant
773	Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi = d\). The representing scheme
774	\(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) is the relative gluing of \(\binom{r}{d}\)
775	affine charts \(U^I\) (each isomorphic to a relative affine space
776	\(\mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_S\)) via the Pl\"ucker cocycle \(\theta_{I,J}\). The

780	\(\det \mathcal{U}\) gives the Pl\"ucker closed embedding into

20	\section{Set-up: charts indexed by $d$-subsets}
21	\label{sec:gr_setup}
22	
23	Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
24	and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
25	for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
26	columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
27	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
28	each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
29	\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
30	matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
31	their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
32	\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).
33	
34	\begin{definition}
35	[Affine chart \(U^I\)]
36	  \label{def:gr_affine_chart}
37	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.affineChart}
38	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
39	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
40	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
41	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
42	  % "If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and
43	  %  $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal
44	  %  to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will
45	  %  mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
46	  %  $I$.
47	  %
48	  %  For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
49	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
50	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
51	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$.
52	  %  Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
53	  %  $x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically
54	  %  isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$."
55	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
56	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). Let \(X^I\) denote the
57	  \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\)
58	  identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\) and whose remaining \(d(r - d)\) entries are
59	  independent indeterminates \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\),
60	  \(q \in \{1,\dots,r\} \setminus I\)) over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Write
61	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) for the polynomial ring in these \(d(r-d)\) variables and
62	  set
63	  \[
64	    U^I \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
65	  \]
66	  The choice of an ordering of the free entries gives a (non-canonical)
67	  isomorphism \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\); in particular
68	  \(U^I\) is a smooth affine \(\mathbb{Z}\)-scheme of relative dimension
69	  \(d(r-d)\). Intuitively a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-point of \(U^I\) is a \(d \times r\)
70	  matrix whose \(I\)-columns form the identity, i.e.\ a rank-\(d\) quotient of
71	  \(\mathbb{Z}^{r}\) trivialised on the coordinates \(I\).
72	\end{definition}
73	
74	\section{Transition maps and the cocycle condition}
75	\label{sec:gr_transition}
76	
77	\begin{definition}
78	[Transition map \(\theta_{I,J}\)]
79	  \label{def:gr_transition}
80	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap}
81	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart}
82	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
83	  % affine patches" (read from
84	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
85	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
86	  % "For any $J\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(J) =d$, let
87	  %  $P^I_J = \det(X^I_J) \in \Z[X^I]$
88	  %  where $X^I_J$ is the $J$ th minor of $X^I$.
89	  %  Let $U^I_J = \spec \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$
90	  %  the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible. This means
91	  %  the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
92	  %  on $U^I_J$.
93	  %
94	  %  For any $I$ and $J$, a ring homomorphism
95	  %  $\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
96	  %  defined as follows. The images of the variables
97	  %  $x^J_{p,q}$ are given by the entries of the matrix formula
98	  %  $\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J) ^{-1}\,X^I$.
99	  %  In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
100	  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
101	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
102	  For \(I, J\) of cardinality \(d\), set
103	  \(P^I_J := \det(X^I_J) \in \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\), the determinant of the \(J\)-th
104	  minor of \(X^I\), and let
105	  \[
106	    U^I_J \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]
107	  \]
108	  be the principal open subscheme of \(U^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) on
109	  which \(P^I_J\) is invertible; over \(U^I_J\) the matrix \(X^I_J\) is
110	  invertible, with inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) whose entries lie in
111	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) by Cramer's rule. Note \(P^I_I = 1\), so
112	  \(U^I_I = U^I\). The \emph{transition map} \(\theta_{I,J}\) is the ring
113	  homomorphism
114	  \[
115	    \theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \longrightarrow
116	    \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J],
117	    \qquad
118	    \theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I,
119	  \]
120	  i.e.\ the indeterminate \(x^J_{p,q}\) maps to the \((p,q)\)-entry of the
121	  \(d \times r\) matrix \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\). This is well defined: the
122	  \(J\)-minor of \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_J = 1_{d\times d}\),
123	  matching the identity \(J\)-block of \(X^J\), and the \(I\)-minor of
124	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\), so
125	  \[
126	    \theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = 1/P^I_J
127	  \]
128	  is invertible in the target; hence \(\theta_{I,J}\) extends from
129	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J]\) to the localisation \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I]\). Dually,
130	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) is the comorphism of a morphism of schemes
131	  \(U^I_J \to U^J_I\), which we also denote \(\theta_{I,J}\); it sends the
132	  matrix \(X^I\) to \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\). One has \(\theta_{I,I} = \id_{U^I}\).
133	\end{definition}
134	
135	\begin{lemma}
136	[Cocycle condition for the transition maps]
137	  \label{lem:gr_cocycle}
138	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.cocycleCondition}
139	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_affine_chart}
140	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
141	  % affine patches" (read from
142	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L838-L848).
143	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
144	  % "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and
145	  %  we leave it to the reader to verify that
146	  %  for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of
147	  %  cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition
148	  %  $\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
149	  %  is satisfied."
150	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
151	  For any three subsets \(I, J, K \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) of cardinality
152	  \(d\), the transition maps of \cref{def:gr_transition} satisfy the cocycle
153	  condition. As morphisms of schemes, on the triple overlap inside \(U^I\),
154	  \[
155	    \theta_{I,K} \;=\; \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J},
156	  \]
157	  equivalently, as ring homomorphisms,
158	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\) (the two read the same
159	  identity through the order-reversal of \(\Spec\)). Together with
160	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\), this is exactly the compatibility datum required to
161	  glue the charts.
162	\end{lemma}
163	
164	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and
165	% we leave it to the reader to verify that
166	% for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of
167	% cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition
168	% $\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
169	% is satisfied."  (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
170	% L838-L848.)
171	\begin{proof}
172	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_affine_chart}
173	  We verify the scheme-morphism form
174	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) by a direct matrix
175	  computation, valid over the open locus of \(U^I\) on which all the minors
176	  involved are invertible (namely \(P^I_J\) and \(P^I_K\), which suffices since
177	  the maps are determined by their restriction to a dense open and all rings are
178	  domains; the computation is an identity of matrices with entries in
179	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J, 1/P^I_K]\)).
180	
181	  Start from the matrix \(X^I\) (the universal point of \(U^I\)). By
182	  \cref{def:gr_transition},
183	  \[
184	    M^J \;:=\; \theta_{I,J}(X^I) \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
185	  \]
186	  a \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(J\)-minor is the identity, hence a point of
187	  \(U^J\). Its \(K\)-minor is
188	  \[
189	    M^J_K \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_K,
190	    \qquad\text{so}\qquad
191	    (M^J_K)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J,
192	  \]
193	  using \((AB)^{-1} = B^{-1} A^{-1}\) for the invertible \(d \times d\) matrices
194	  \(X^I_J\) and \(X^I_K\). Applying \(\theta_{J,K}\) to \(M^J\) gives
195	  \[
196	    \theta_{J,K}(M^J) \;=\; (M^J_K)^{-1} M^J
197	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J \,\cdot\, (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I
198	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I,
199	  \]
200	  the middle product \(X^I_J (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\) cancelling by
201	  associativity of matrix multiplication. The right-hand side is precisely
202	  \(\theta_{I,K}(X^I) = (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I\). Thus
203	  \(\theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,K}\) agree on the
204	  universal matrix \(X^I\), hence agree as morphisms \(U^I \supseteq U^I_J \cap
205	  U^I_K \to U^K\). The special case \(K = I\) (resp.\ \(J = I\)) together with
206	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\) shows each \(\theta_{I,J}\) is an isomorphism onto its
207	  image with inverse \(\theta_{J,I}\), since
208	  \(\theta_{J,I} \circ \theta_{I,J} = \theta_{I,I} = \id\).
209	\end{proof}

211	\section{The glued Grassmannian scheme}
212	\label{sec:gr_glued}
213	
214	\begin{definition}
215	[The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
216	  \label{def:gr_glued_scheme}
217	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scheme}
218	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
219	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
220	  % affine patches" (read from
221	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L843-L850).
222	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
223	  % "Therefore the schemes
224	  %  $U^I$, as $I$ varies over all the
225	  %  ${r\choose d}$ different
226	  %  subsets of  $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of cardinality $d$,
227	  %  can be glued together by the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$ to form a
228	  %  finite-type scheme $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$. As each $U^I$ is
229	  %  isomorphic to $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$, it follows that
230	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is smooth of relative dimension $d(r-d)$."
231	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
232	  Gluing the affine charts \(\{U^I\}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), one for each
233	  of the \(\binom{r}{d}\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) of cardinality
234	  \(d\), along the open subschemes \(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\) via the transition
235	  isomorphisms \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I_J \xrightarrow{\sim} U^J_I\)
236	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}) -- which satisfy the cocycle condition
237	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\)
238	  of \cref{lem:gr_cocycle} -- yields a scheme
239	  \[
240	    \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \longrightarrow \Spec \mathbb{Z}.
241	  \]
242	  It is of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\) (a finite gluing of finitely
243	  generated \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebras), and under the gluing each \(U^I\) is an
244	  open subscheme of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J\).
245	
246	  Moreover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is \emph{smooth of relative
247	  dimension \(d(r-d)\)}. Smoothness and relative dimension are local on the
248	  source; the charts \(\{U^I\}\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and each
249	  \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) is
250	  affine space of dimension \(d(r-d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\), which is smooth over
251	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\) (the polynomial ring
252	  \(\mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_{d(r-d)}]\) is a smooth \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra). As
253	  the property may be checked on an open cover of the source, the conclusion
254	  follows.
255	\end{definition}

257	\section{Separatedness}
258	\label{sec:gr_separated}
259	
260	\begin{lemma}
261	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
262	  \label{lem:gr_separated}
263	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isSeparated}
264	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
265	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Separatedness" (read from
266	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860).
267	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
268	  % "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with $U^I\times U^J$
269	  %  can be seen to be the closed subscheme $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$
270	  %  defined by entries of the matrix formula $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$,
271	  %  and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
272	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
273	  The structure morphism \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
274	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is separated; equivalently, the diagonal
275	  \(\Delta : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \times_{\mathbb{Z}}
276	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is a closed immersion.
277	\end{lemma}
278	
279	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with
280	% $U^I\times U^J$ can be seen to be the closed subscheme
281	% $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
282	% $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$, and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
283	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860.)
284	\begin{proof}
285	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
286	  The products \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), as \(I, J\) range over the
287	  size-\(d\) subsets, form an affine open cover of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)
288	  \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Separatedness may be checked on this
289	  cover: it suffices to show that for each pair \((I,J)\) the preimage
290	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J)\) maps by a closed immersion into
291	  \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), i.e.\ that the corresponding ring
292	  homomorphism is surjective.
293	
294	  By \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme} the diagonal preimage is the overlap
295	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J) = U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J = \Spec
296	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\). The restricted diagonal corresponds to the ring map
297	  \[
298	    \delta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}[X^J]
299	    \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J],
300	    \qquad
301	    X^I \otimes 1 \mapsto X^I, \quad 1 \otimes X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
302	  \]
303	  the second component being the comorphism \(\theta_{I,J}\) of
304	  \cref{def:gr_transition} (a point of \(U^I_J\) and its image under
305	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) are the two coordinates of a point of the diagonal). We claim
306	  \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Its image contains all entries of \(X^I\)
307	  (from the first factor). From the second factor it contains the entries of
308	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\); taking the \(I\)-minor of this matrix, and using
309	  \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\), the image contains the entries of \((X^I_J)^{-1}\),
310	  and in particular
311	  \[
312	    \delta_{I,J}(1 \otimes P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = 1/P^I_J .
313	  \]
314	  Hence \(1/P^I_J\) lies in the image, so the image is all of
315	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) and \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Concretely the
316	  closed subscheme \(\Delta_{I,J} \subseteq U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\) is cut
317	  out by the entries of the matrix relation
318	  \[
319	    X^J_I \, X^I - X^J \;=\; 0,
320	  \]
321	  which on the diagonal holds because \(X^J = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) forces
322	  \(X^J_I = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) and therefore \(X^J_I X^I =
323	  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I = X^J\). As each restricted diagonal is a closed immersion,
324	  \(\Delta\) is a closed immersion and \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over
325	  \(\mathbb{Z}\).
326	\end{proof}

329	\label{sec:gr_proper}

331	\begin{lemma}
332	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is proper over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
333	  \label{lem:gr_proper}
334	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isProper}
335	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_separated}

390	\begin{proof}
391	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_separated, def:gr_transition}
392	  Since \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is of finite type
393	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) and separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), and
394	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) is Noetherian, properness follows from the valuative criterion
395	  for discrete valuation rings: it suffices to show that every solid diagram
396	  \[
397	    \begin{array}{ccc}
398	      \Spec K & \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } & \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \\
399	      \downarrow & & \downarrow \pi \\
400	      \Spec R & \longrightarrow & \Spec \mathbb{Z}
401	    \end{array}
402	  \]
403	  with \(R\) a discrete valuation ring, \(K = \operatorname{Frac}(R)\) and
404	  valuation \(\nu : K \to \mathbb{Z} \cup \{\infty\}\), admits a unique diagonal
405	  filler \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
406	
407	  \emph{Existence.} The morphism \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) lands
408	  in some chart \(U^I\) (\(\Spec K\) has one point), so it is given by a ring map
409	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\). Among the \(\binom{r}{d}\) minors choose \(J\)
410	  with \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) minimal. Because \(P^I_I = 1\) gives \(f(P^I_I) = 1\)
411	  with \(\nu = 0\), the minimum satisfies \(\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0\); in particular
412	  \(f(P^I_J) \ne 0\), so \(f(X^I_J) \in \mathrm{GL}_d(K)\) is invertible over
413	  \(K\). Define
414	  \[
415	    g : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \longrightarrow K, \qquad
416	    g(X^J) = f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\bigr) ;
417	  \]
418	  this is the composite of \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) with
419	  \(f\), so \(g\) defines the same \(K\)-point \(\varphi\), now exhibited as
420	  landing in \(U^J\).
421	
422	  We show every entry \(g(x^J_{p,q})\) lies in \(R\), i.e.\ has \(\nu \ge 0\).
423	  First, for any size-\(d\) subset \(K' \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) the \(K'\)-minor
424	  of \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\), so
425	  \[
426	    g(P^J_{K'}) = \det f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\bigr)
427	    = \frac{f(P^I_{K'})}{f(P^I_J)},
428	    \qquad
429	    \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) = \nu(f(P^I_{K'})) - \nu(f(P^I_J)) \ge 0
430	  \]
431	  by minimality of \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\). Now the \(J\)-minor \(X^J_J\) of \(X^J\) is
432	  the identity, so by cofactor expansion each individual entry
433	  \(x^J_{p,q}\) (with \(q \notin J\)) equals, up to sign, the minor
434	  \(P^J_{K'}\) for \(K' = (J \setminus \{j_p\}) \cup \{q\}\) -- the \(d \times d\)
435	  determinant obtained by replacing the \(p\)-th identity column of \(X^J_J\) by
436	  the column \(q\). Hence \(\nu(g(x^J_{p,q})) = \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) \ge 0\) for all
437	  entries, and the entries with \(q \in J\) are \(0\) or \(1\). Therefore
438	  \(g(\mathbb{Z}[X^J]) \subseteq R\), so \(g\) factors uniquely as
439	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R \hookrightarrow K\). The corresponding morphism
440	  \(\Spec R \to U^J \hookrightarrow \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) restricts to \(\varphi\)
441	  over \(\Spec K\) and lies over \(\Spec \mathbb{Z}\), providing the filler.
442	
443	  \emph{Uniqueness.} Any two fillers agree over the generic point \(\Spec K\)
444	  and, \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) being separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)
445	  (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), two morphisms \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
446	  agreeing on the schematically dense open \(\Spec K\) coincide. This verifies
447	  the valuative criterion, so \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is
448	  proper.
449	\end{proof}

451	\section{Out of scope}
452	\label{sec:gr_out_of_scope}
453	
454	This chapter constructs the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
455	the absolute base \(\mathbb{Z}\) and establishes its smoothness, separatedness
456	and properness -- the GR-cells and GR-glue sub-phases. The following are
457	deliberately deferred to later sub-phases and are not treated here:
458	\begin{itemize}
459	  \item \textbf{The tautological/universal quotient} \(u : \mathcal{O}^{\oplus
460	    r}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) and the determinant
461	    line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\) with its Pl\"ucker embedding (GR-quot).
462	  \item \textbf{The functor-of-points representability} of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
463	    -- that it represents \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d) =
464	    \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^r \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}} /
465	    \mathbb{Z} / \mathbb{Z}}\) (GR-repr); this is blueprinted as
466	    \cref{thm:grassmannian_representable} in
467	    \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}.
468	  \item \textbf{The relative version over a general base \(S\)} with a locally
469	    free sheaf \(V\) of rank \(r\): the scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V,d)\) obtained by
470	    base change and gluing over a trivialising cover of \(V\). The
471	    absolute-over-\(\mathbb{Z}\) construction of this chapter is the input to
472	    that base change.
473	\end{itemize}

446	\bigskip
447	
448	\centerline{\large\bf Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials}
449	
450	\medskip
451	
452	
453	Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
454	a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
455	Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on 
456	$X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
457	the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
458	the function 
459	$${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) = 
460	\sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
461	where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the 
462	coherence and properness conditions. The fact that $\chi(F(m))$
463	is indeed a polynomial in $m$ under the above assumption is a special
464	case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
465	
466	
467	
468	Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
469	and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
470	be any coherent sheaf on 
471	$X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
472	we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$ 
473	which is the Hilbert polynomial of
474	the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
475	calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$. 
476	If $\F$ is flat over $S$ then the function $s\mapsto {\Phi}_s$ 
477	from the set of points of $S$ to the polynomial ring $\Q[\lambda]$ is 
478	known to be locally constant on $S$. 
479	
480	
481	This shows that the functor $\Quot_{E/X/S}$ naturally decomposes
482	as a co-product 
483	$$\Quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$$
484	where for any polynomial ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$, the functor 
485	$\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ associates
486	to any $T$ the set of all equivalence classes of 
487	families $\langle \F,q \rangle$ such that at each $t\in T$ the 
488	Hilbert polynomial of the restriction $\F_t$, calculated
489	using the pull-back of $L$, is ${\Phi}$. Correspondingly,
490	the representing scheme  $\quot_{E/X/S}$, when it exists, naturally decomposes
491	as a co-product
492	$$\quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$$
493	
494	\textbf{Note } 
495	We will generally take $X$ to be (quasi-)projective over $S$,
496	and $L$ to be a relatively very ample line bundle. Then indeed 
497	the Hilbert and Quot functors are representable by schemes,
498	but not in general.
499	
500	
501	
502	\medskip
503	
504	
505	\bigskip
506	
507	\centerline{\large\bf Elementary Examples, Exercises}
508	
509	
510	\medskip
511	
512	\textbf{(1) } {\bf $\PP^n_{\Z}$ as a Quot scheme } 
513	Show that the scheme $\PP^n_{\Z} = \proj \Z[x_0,\ldots,x_n]$ represents the 
514	functor $\varphi$ from schemes to sets, which associates
515	to any $S$ the set of all equivalence classes $\langle \F, q\rangle$ 
516	of quotients
517	$q: \oplus^{n+1}{\mathcal O}_S \to \F$, where $\F$ is an invertible ${\mathcal O}_S$-module. 
518	As coherent sheaves on $S$ which are ${\mathcal O}_S$-flat with each
519	fiber $1$-dimensional are exactly the locally free sheaves on $S$ 
520	of rank $1$, it follows that $\varphi$ is the functor 
521	$\Quot_{\oplus^{n+1}{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{1, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$
522	(where in some places we write just $\Z$ for $\spec \Z$ for simplicity). 
523	This shows that
524	$\quot_{\oplus^{n+1}{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{1, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}} 
525	= \PP^n_{\Z}$. 
526	Under this identification, show that the universal family on
527	$\quot_{\oplus^{n+1}{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{1, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$ 
528	is the tautological quotient 
529	$\oplus^{n+1}{\mathcal O}_{\PP^n_{\Z}} \to {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n_{\Z}}(1)$
530	 
531	More generally, show that if $E$ is a locally free sheaf on a 
532	noetherian scheme $S$, the functor $\Quot_{E/S/S}^{1, {\mathcal O}_S}$ is represented 
533	by the $S$-scheme $\PP(E) = \Proj \sym_{{\mathcal O}_S} E$, 
534	with the tautological quotient 
535	$\pi^*(E) \to {\mathcal O}_{\PP(E)}(1)$ as the universal family.
536

382	It is immediate that the functor $\Quot_{\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}}$
383	satisfies faithfully flat descent. It was proved by
384	Grothendieck that in fact the above functor
385	is representable on the category of all locally noetherian schemes 
386	by a scheme $\quot_{\oplus^r{\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}}$.

389	\medskip

393	
394	\centerline{\large\bf The Functors $\Hilb_{X/S}$ and $\Quot_{E/X/S}$}
395	
396	\medskip
397	
398	The above functors $\Hilb_{\PP^n}$ and $\Quot_{\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}}$
399	admit the following simple generalisations. 
400	Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme and let $X\to S$ be a finite type
401	scheme over it. Let $E$ be a coherent sheaf on $X$. 
402	Let $Sch_S$ denote the category of all locally noetherian schemes
403	over $S$. For any $T\to S$ in $Sch_S$, a \textbf{family of quotients of $E$ 
404	parametrised by $T$} will mean 
405	a pair $(\F,q)$ consisting of 
406	
407	(i) a coherent sheaf $\F$ on $X_T = X\times_ST$ 
408	such that the schematic support of $\F$ is proper over $T$ 
409	and $\F$ is flat over $T$, together with 
410	
411	(ii) a surjective ${\mathcal O}_{X_T}$-linear homomorphism of sheaves 
412	$q:E_T  \to \F$ where $E_T$ is the pull-back of $E$ under
413	the projection $X_T \to X$.
414	
415	Two such families $(\F,q)$ and $(\F,q)$ parametrised by $T$ will 
416	be regarded as equivalent if $\ker(q) = \ker(q')$), 
417	and $\langle \F, q \rangle$ will denote 
418	an equivalence class. Then as properness and flatness are preserved
419	by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact, 
420	the pull-back of $\langle \F, q \rangle$ under an $S$-morphism $T'\to T$ is
421	well-defined, which gives
422	a set-valued contravariant functor 
423	$\Quot_{E/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets$ under which   
424	$$T\mapsto  \{\mbox{ All } \langle \F, q \rangle \mbox{ parametrised by }T\}$$
425	
426	When $E = {\mathcal O}_X$, the functor $\Quot_{{\mathcal O}_X/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets$ 
427	associates to $T$ the set of all closed subschemes $Y\subset X_T$
428	that are proper and flat over $T$. We denote this functor by $\Hilb_{X/S}$.
429	
430	Note in particular that we have
431	$$\Hilb_{\PP^n} = \Hilb_{\PP^n_{\Z}/\spec \Z} \mbox{ and }
432	\Quot_{\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}} = 
433	\Quot_{\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n_{\Z}}/\PP^n_{\Z}/\spec \Z}$$

436	It is clear that the functors $\Quot_{E/X/S}$ and $\Hilb_{X/S}$

\textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
  Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a category and \(F : \mathcal{C}^{op} \to \mathbf{Set}\)
  a presheaf of sets. The predicate \(F.\mathrm{IsRepresentable}\) asserts that
  \(F\) is representable. It unfolds -- via
  \texttt{Functor.IsRepresentable.has\_representation} -- to exactly
  \[
    \exists\,Y,\;\; \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(F.\mathrm{RepresentableBy}\,Y\bigr),
  \]
  i.e.\ the existence of an object \(Y\) of \(\mathcal{C}\) together with a
  natural bijection \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathcal{C}}(-, Y) \cong F\). When it holds,
  \texttt{Functor.representableBy} produces the canonical witness
  \(F.\mathrm{RepresentableBy}\,(F.\mathrm{reprX})\) on the representing object
  \(F.\mathrm{reprX}\); the equivalence \texttt{Functor.representableByEquiv}
  identifies any such witness \(F.\mathrm{RepresentableBy}\,Y\) with the natural
  isomorphism \(\mathrm{yoneda}(Y) \cong F\), i.e.\ the Yoneda bijection
  \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathcal{C}}(-, Y) \cong F\).
